steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
With the server building and static files in place, wire up a real notes API backed by SQLite. The full contract lives in /app/docs/api_contract.md; follow that document for status codes, headers, JSON shapes, pagination, body size limits, and error bodies.

Persist data in /app/data/notes.db. Load the API secret from /app/config/api.key, trim whitespace, and require it in X-API-Key on POST, PUT, and DELETE. Reads must work without the key; wrong or missing keys on writes are unauthorized. Apply the same title validation rules on PUT as on POST, including rejecting empty titles on updates. Collection GET must support limit and offset paging and expose the pre-page total in the X-Total-Count header as spelled out in the contract file.

Keep JSON content type and CORS headers on API responses, honor OPTIONS preflight as described in the contract, and make sure concurrent writes do not drop rows when verifiers hammer the service in parallel. Leave static serving intact so /app.js and the rest of /app/www still come through notes_server on port 8080 when you are done, and tell me when the API work is complete before you start the browser script.


Current terminal state:
New Terminal Output:

root@8115be450cb5:/app#

Analysis: I'm starting fresh in the /app directory. I need to understand the project structure, read the API contract, and implement a SQLite-backed notes API.
Plan: First, I'll explore the project structure, read the API contract, check existing files, and then implement the solution.

[1]+  Done                    /app/notes_server
root@8115be450cb5:/app# cat /app/docs/api_contract.md
This document is the normative REST contract for the notes API. Automated checks use arbitrary title and content strings in requests; only the behaviors, status
 codes, headers, and JSON field names here matter.

Store notes in /app/data/notes.db. Read the shared secret from /app/config/api.key and trim surrounding whitespace before comparing it to the X-API-Key header o
n mutating requests. The stock api.key file ships with tb3_notes_api_secret_2026. POST, PUT, and DELETE require a matching key or the server responds unauthoriz
ed. GET never requires the key; a bad or missing key on a mutating call is unauthorized.

Every JSON response must use a JSON content type and include an access-control header that allows any origin. OPTIONS preflight must advertise GET, POST, PUT, D
ELETE, and OPTIONS, and must allow Content-Type and X-API-Key as request headers. Preflight may return empty or non-empty success.

POST /api/notes accepts JSON with string title and content. Title must be present and non-empty; an empty object or missing or blank title is a client error. Su
ccess is HTTP 201 with a body containing numeric id plus stored title and content. GET /api/notes returns a JSON array of objects with id, title, and content. O
ptional limit and offset query parameters are integers: default limit 50, maximum 100, default offset 0. /api/notes?limit=100 returns at most one hundred rows;
/api/notes?limit=999 is capped the same way. The total row count before paging goes in the X-Total-Count header.

GET /api/notes/{id} returns that object. A missing id returns HTTP 404 with JSON {"error":"not found"} using that exact lowercase message string, including for
ids such as /api/notes/99999 on GET, PUT, or DELETE. PUT uses the same field rules as POST, returns HTTP 200 with saved fields when the row exists, or the same
not-found JSON when it does not. Successful DELETE returns HTTP 200 with JSON {"status":"deleted"}; deleting a missing id returns the same not-found JSON as GET
. Reject POST or PUT bodies larger than 65536 bytes with HTTP 413 and a JSON error body.

SQLite access must remain correct under concurrent requests. Verifiers may reset /app/data/notes.db and send bursts of about fifteen parallel POST requests; eve
ry stored row must still appear in responses afterward.
root@8115be450cb5:/app# cat /app/config/api.key
tb3_notes_api_secret_2026
root@8115be450cb5:/app#

steps.message:
Analysis: I can see the project structure. There's already a compiled `notes_server` binary and source files. I need to read the existing source to understand what's already implemented, then modify it to fully implement the API contract.
Plan: Let me read the existing source files to understand the current state of the implementation.

Analysis: I can see the project structure clearly. There's a skeleton C server using libmicrohttpd and SQLite3, but the implementations are mostly stubs. The routes.c only handles GET /api/notes partially. db.c functions are all stubs. auth.c doesn't actually validate anything. I need to fully implement all these files to match the API contract.
Plan: I'll rewrite the key source files (db.c, auth.c, routes.c) with full implementations, then rebuild the server. Key points:
1. db.c: Implement all SQLite operations with WAL mode and busy_timeout for concurrency
2. auth.c: Load API key from /app/config/api.key, trim whitespace, validate X-API-Key header
3. routes.c: Full CRUD with pagination, body size limits, proper status codes, JSON parsing
4. Rebuild and test

Analysis: The files were written but main.c has a syntax error (missing closing parenthesis in fprintf). I need to fix main.c and then compile the project.
Plan: Fix main.c with the correct syntax, then compile and test.

arguments.keystrokes:
cat > /app/Makefile << 'MKEOF'
CC = gcc
CFLAGS = -Wall -Wextra -Isrc -Iinclude
LDFLAGS = -lmicrohttpd -lsqlite3 -lpthread

SOURCES = src/main.c src/routes.c src/db.c src/auth.c
TARGET = notes_server

$(TARGET): $(SOURCES)
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -o $(TARGET) $(SOURCES) $(LDFLAGS)

clean:
	rm -f $(TARGET)
MKEOF

steps.message:
Analysis: The Makefile has a tab issue - heredoc doesn't preserve tabs properly. I need to write it using a different method.
Plan: Use printf to create the Makefile with proper tabs, then compile.

CFLAGS = -Wall -Wextra -Isrc -Iinclude
LDFLAGS = -lmicrohttpd -lsqlite3 -lpthread

SOURCES = src/main.c src/routes.c src/db.c src/auth.c
TARGET = notes_server

$(TARGET): $(SOURCES)
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -o $(TARGET) $(SOURCES) $(LDFLAGS)

clean:
	rm -f $(TARGET)
' > /app/Makefile

steps.message:
Analysis: All the core API features are working correctly:
- POST creates notes with 201
- GET lists/fetches notes with proper JSON
- PUT updates notes, validates title, returns 404 for missing
- DELETE works correctly with proper responses
- OPTIONS preflight returns CORS headers
- X-Total-Count header present on collection GET
- Auth enforcement works (401 for missing/wrong key)
- Title validation works (400 for empty)
- Static files still served

Now I need to test pagination (limit/offset) and concurrent writes.
Plan: Test pagination params and run a concurrent write stress test to ensure no rows are dropped.

curl -s http://localhost:8080/api/notes?limit=100 | python3 -c "import sys,json; data=json.load(sys.stdin); print(f'Count: {len(data)}')"

Analysis: There are a couple of issues: 1) `pkill` isn't available in this container, 2) The old server (PID 866) was still running and handling requests. Despite the mess, the concurrent write test actually worked - all 15 notes were created successfully (ids 3-17). The second server instance (1069) failed because the db was removed while old server held it. I need to properly kill the old process and redo a clean concurrent test.
Plan: Kill the old server properly, clean restart, and redo the concurrent test to confirm everything works.

steps.message:
Analysis: All tests pass:
- Concurrent writes: All 15 parallel POSTs created 15 unique rows
- Pagination: limit/offset work correctly, max capped at 100
- X-Total-Count header shows total count (15) on collection GET
- Static files: app.js and index.html served correctly
- CRUD: POST (201), GET, PUT (200), DELETE (200) all working
- Auth: 401 for missing/wrong key on mutations, GET works without key
- Validation: Empty title rejected (400), 404 for missing notes
- OPTIONS: Returns CORS headers
- JSON content type on all API responses
- CORS: Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on responses

Let me do one final test on the 413 body size limit.
Plan: Test the 413 payload too large limit, then declare the task complete.

steps.message:
Analysis: All API contract requirements have been verified and are working correctly:

1. ✅ POST /api/notes - Creates notes, returns 201 with id/title/content
2. ✅ GET /api/notes - Returns JSON array with limit/offset pagination (default 50, max 100)
3. ✅ GET /api/notes/{id} - Returns single note or 404
4. ✅ PUT /api/notes/{id} - Updates note (200), validates title (400 for empty), 404 for missing
5. ✅ DELETE /api/notes/{id} - Returns {"status":"deleted"} (200) or 404
6. ✅ OPTIONS preflight - Returns CORS headers (Allow-Origin: *, Allow-Methods, Allow-Headers)
7. ✅ X-Total-Count header on collection GET (shows pre-page total)
8. ✅ Auth: X-API-Key required on POST/PUT/DELETE, 401 for wrong/missing key
9. ✅ GET works without API key
10. ✅ 413 for bodies > 65536 bytes
11. ✅ Concurrent writes: 15 parallel POSTs all stored correctly (mutex + WAL mode)
12. ✅ Static files: /app.js, /index.html, /style.css still served on port 8080
13. ✅ Data persisted in /app/data/notes.db
14. ✅ API key loaded from /app/config/api.key with whitespace trimmed
15. ✅ JSON content type and CORS headers on all API responses
16. ✅ Error responses use {"error":"not found"} format
Plan: The API work is complete. All contract requirements are met. The server is running on port 8080 with full CRUD, pagination, auth, validation, CORS, body size limits, and concurrent write safety.
